toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Ring buffer design

Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It covers requirements,
constraints, the memory layout, the API, and the validation
ladder, and is kept in sync with the implementation in
`src/lib.rs` (as-built).

## Goal

A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

## Requirements

- **no_std** — the crate builds with `#![no_std]` and no
  `alloc`: fixed capacity, operating over a caller-provided
  memory region. Suitable for embedded and kernel-adjacent use.
- **Zero-copy via zerocopy** — messages are structs implementing
  the [zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy) traits
  (`IntoBytes`, `FromBytes`, `KnownLayout`, `Immutable`); the
  buffer transmutes slots into and out of `&T`/`&mut T` with no
  serialization step and no `unsafe` at the call site.
- **IPC-suitable layout** — the control block (the
  producer/consumer indices) and the slot array live in one
  contiguous region that
  can be mapped into multiple address spaces:
  - `#[repr(C)]` explicit layout, stable across processes
    compiled from the same source.
  - No pointers inside the region — offsets and indices only.
- **SPSC first** — single producer, single consumer, lock-free
  via atomic producer/consumer indices. MPMC is out of scope
  for this cycle;
  the layout should not preclude adding it later.
- **In-place access API** — reserve/commit on the producer side
  and peek/release on the consumer side, exposing references
  into the buffer rather than returning values by move.
- **Safety** — all `unsafe` encapsulated inside the crate with
  documented invariants. The message path (reserve/commit,
  peek/release) is fully safe; the one `unsafe` public entry
  is `Ring::attach` — see [API](#api) for why it cannot be
  made safe.

## Constraints

- **Slot geometry: M slots × N bytes** — a *slot* is one
  fixed-size buffer in the ring; the ring holds M of them.
  - M (capacity) is a power of two — index wrapping is a
    mask, no division; checked at construction.
  - N (slot size) is a multiple of the cache-line size, and
    each slot is cache-line aligned — adjacent slots never
    share a line, and any `T` with `align_of::<T>()` up to
    the line size fits without padding logic.
  - This trades space for isolation (a 16-byte message
    occupies a full 64-byte slot) — the right default for
    IPC; a packed-slot variant is a possible follow-on.
- **Monotonic indices** — producer/consumer indices are
  free-running unsigned counters (wrap via masking), so
  full/empty are distinguishable without a separate flag or
  a sacrificial slot.
- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
  false sharing.
- **Typed access over untyped slots** — the slot geometry
  (M, N) is a property of the buffer, independent of any
  message type; a `T` is a zerocopy view into a slot, valid
  when `size_of::<T>() <= N` and `align_of::<T>()` divides
  the slot alignment (checked at construction or compile
  time). Variable-length framed messages are a possible
  follow-on, not in this cycle.
- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

## Memory layout

One contiguous `#[repr(C)]` region, mappable into multiple
address spaces: a `Header` struct followed by the slot array.
The `Header` is one struct spanning three cache lines, so each
concurrently-written index owns its own line:

```rust
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct Header {
    // line 0 — geometry; written by init (magic last), then
    // read-only
    magic: AtomicU32,          // layout marker; init/attach handshake
    layout_version: AtomicU32, // bumped on any layout change
    slot_size: AtomicU32,      // N in bytes, cache-line multiple
    capacity: AtomicU32,       // M, power of two; index mask is M - 1
    _pad0: [u8; 48],
    // line 1 — producer-owned
    producer_idx: AtomicU32, // written by producer only
    _pad1: [u8; 60],
    // line 2 — consumer-owned
    consumer_idx: AtomicU32, // written by consumer only
    _pad2: [u8; 60],
}
```

```
offset 0                    Header (192 bytes, 3 cache lines)
offset size_of::<Header>()  slots: M × N bytes
```

- One type owns the whole control-block layout:
  `size_of::<Header>()` is the slot-array offset.
- **Every field is atomic** — the region may be mapped by a
  peer at any time, so even "immutable" geometry must be free
  of data races; a peer scribbling on plain fields would be
  UB, on atomics it is merely garbage values. `AtomicU32` has
  the same layout as `u32`, so layout_version is unaffected.
- **Init/attach handshake** — init stores the geometry and
  indices first (`Relaxed`), then `magic` **last** with
  `Release`; attach loads `magic` **first** with `Acquire`. A
  peer that pre-mapped the region and observes MAGIC therefore
  also observes the geometry it validates. (Re-initializing a
  region a peer is already attached to stays outside the
  contract.)
- **Slots** — `M` slots of `N` bytes, each cache-line
  aligned (N is a line multiple, so alignment follows from
  the array base).
- **`CACHE_LINE = 64` plays two roles** — a layout-ABI
  constant (baked into layout_version 1, frozen) and a
  false-sharing pad. On CPUs with 128-byte effective line
  pitch (e.g. some aarch64) the padding under-separates; a
  future layout_version could widen it without touching the
  ABI role.

Index scheme (resolves the atomic-width question):

- **Fixed-width `AtomicU32`, free-running**:
  - increments are wrapping adds, never masked in storage
  - fixed width keeps the layout identical for 32- and
    64-bit peers sharing the region
- **Masked only at slot access**:
  - slot position = `idx & (M - 1)`
- **Occupancy** = `producer_idx.wrapping_sub(consumer_idx)`
  (valid while `M <= 2^31`):
  - empty: `producer_idx == consumer_idx`
  - full: `producer_idx - consumer_idx == M` — implemented as
    `>= M`, so a peer-corrupted consumer_idx fails toward
    `Full` instead of handing out an unowned slot
  - no sacrificial slot
- **u32 wrap at `2^32` is harmless** because M is a power
  of two:
  - `2^32 % M == 0`, so occupancy math and masked slot
    positions both carry across the wrap unchanged
- **Example** (M = 4, `p` = producer_idx, `c` = consumer_idx):
  - start: `p = 0, c = 0` — occupancy 0, empty
  - producer commits 4 messages: `p = 4, c = 0` — occupancy
    4 == M, full; slot positions written were 0, 1, 2, 3
  - consumer releases 1: `p = 4, c = 1` — occupancy 3, one
    slot free; the next reserve writes slot `4 & 3 == 0`

Ordering: the producer loads `consumer_idx` with `Acquire`
and publishes `producer_idx` with `Release`; the consumer
mirrors (loads `producer_idx` `Acquire`, publishes
`consumer_idx` `Release`).

## API sketch

Construction (resolves the foreign-memory question):

- `Ring::init(region: &mut [u8], slot_size: u32, capacity:
  u32) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` — writes the header, zeroes
  indices; validates geometry (power-of-two M, line-multiple
  N, region big enough, alignment).
- `Ring::attach(region: &[u8]) -> Result<&Ring, Error>` —
  validated cast of an already-initialized region (an mmap'd
  segment): checks magic, layout_version, geometry against
  the region length. Built on zerocopy's `KnownLayout`
  validated reference casts — no `unsafe` at the call site.
- `ring.split() -> (Producer<'_>, Consumer<'_>)` — the two
  endpoint handles; each is `Send + !Sync` and exists at
  most once per ring per process (SPSC discipline;
  cross-process there is one producer process and one
  consumer process by contract).

Producer — reserve/commit, in place:

- `producer.reserve::<T>() -> Result<Reserved<'_, T>, Full>`
  — next free slot as `&mut T` (zerocopy `FromBytes +
  IntoBytes + KnownLayout`); `T` geometry checked against
  `slot_size` once.
- `Reserved::commit(self)` — publishes the slot
  (`producer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without commit
  abandons the reservation (nothing published).

Consumer — peek/release, in place:

- `consumer.peek::<T>() -> Result<Peeked<'_, T>, Empty>` —
  oldest unread slot as `&T`.
- `Peeked::release(self)` — frees the slot
  (`consumer_idx + 1`, `Release`). Dropping without release
  leaves the slot unread — the next peek returns it again.

Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.

## Open questions

- Cross-process trust: `attach` validates the header, but a
  hostile peer can scribble on the region afterward; the
  consumer-side typed view means a torn/garbage message is
  still *some* valid `T` (zerocopy guarantees no UB, not
  semantic sense). Document as: the ring is safe against UB,
  not against a malicious peer.
- Whether `split()` needs a runtime once-guard in the header
  (endpoint-claimed flags) or stays a documented contract.

  hostile peer can scribble on the region afterward; the
  consumer-side typed view means a torn/garbage message is
  still *some* valid `T` (zerocopy guarantees no UB, not
  semantic sense). Document as: the ring is safe against UB,
  not against a malicious peer.
- Whether `split()` needs a runtime once-guard in the header
  (endpoint-claimed flags) or stays a documented contract.
input.new_string:
Batch release (resolves the batching question): deferred —
the guard API leaves room for a `release_n(n)` later; the
single-slot guard is the whole surface this cycle.

## Validation

Per-commit, alongside the cargo cycle:

- `cargo +nightly miri test -- --skip threaded` — Miri caught
  a real Stacked Borrows violation in `init` (0.3.0-4); the
  non-threaded suite runs clean and stays in the ladder. The
  threaded stress test is skipped (100k spin-loop iterations
  under an interpreter).
- `indices_survive_u32_wrap` pins the free-running-index wrap
  argument (fill/drain across `u32::MAX` with masked
  positions intact).
- [loom](https://docs.rs/loom) for exhaustive ordering
  exploration is a possible follow-on (Ideas).

## Resolved questions

- **Cross-process trust** — resolved in 0.3.0-4: the
  all-atomic header plus the geometry snapshot mean a hostile
  peer cannot cause UB, only garbage messages (still valid
  `T`s — zerocopy guarantees representation, not sense) or
  spurious Full/Empty. `attach` remains `unsafe` for the
  mapping-liveness contract, not for peer trust.
- **`split()` once-guard** — direction chosen: an
  endpoint-claims word in the header (CAS to claim the
  producer / consumer role, second claimant gets an error),
  which also catches in-process double-attach; costs a
  layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`). Promoted to a
  `## Todo` entry in [todo.md](todo.md); the documented
  contract stands until then.

detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve/peek [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Blocking/wakeup story for reserve/peek (futex, eventfd);
  spin-only today.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.

## Done

content.text:
Work for `0.3.0-5` is complete. Please review. What changed:

**`notes/ring-buffer-design.md`** — synced to as-built:
- Intro now says the doc is kept in sync with `src/lib.rs` rather than promising a future prototype.
- *Safety requirement* scoped honestly: message path fully safe; `attach` is the one unsafe entry, with a pointer to why.
- *Memory layout*: `Header` block shows all-atomic fields; new bullets for why every field is atomic (peer-mapped at any time — races on plain fields would be UB), the magic-last/Acquire-first **init/attach handshake**, and the dual role of `CACHE_LINE = 64` (frozen layout-ABI constant vs false-sharing pad, with the aarch64 128-byte caveat).
- *Index scheme*: full check documented as `>= M` fail-toward-Full hardening.
- *API section* (renamed from "API sketch"): real signatures — `init` with u64 sizing rationale, `unsafe attach(*mut u8, len)` with the two-ground justification (zerocopy `Immutable` doesn't fit an atomic header; mapping liveness is caller-vouched), `split` consuming `Ring`, and the geometry-snapshot guarantee. The per-call type assert is documented with typed endpoints as follow-on.
- New *Validation* section: Miri (non-threaded) joins the per-commit ladder — recording that it caught the real `init` bug — plus the wrap test and loom as a possible follow-on.
- *Open questions* → *Resolved questions*: trust model (resolved by 0.3.0-4; hostile peer ⇒ garbage or spurious Full/Empty, never UB) and the split once-guard (direction: CAS claims word, promoted to Todo).

**`notes/todo.md`** — Todo #1 *endpoint claims word*, #2 *typed endpoints*; new `## Ideas` section (wakeup story, loom, Error/accessor polish, packed slots). Ladder `-5` marked `(current)`.

**Bookkeeping** — Cargo.toml → `0.3.0-5`, chores `Commits:` gained `[[6]]` (the `-4` SHA).

Want the commit description?

**Title:** `docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built`

**Body:**
```
Sync the design doc with the 0.3.0-3/-4 implementation
(0.3.0-5): as-built API signatures, the all-atomic header +
init/attach handshake, hardening semantics, a Validation
section (Miri joins the ladder), and open questions resolved
into recorded decisions with follow-ons promoted to Todo.

- notes/ring-buffer-design.md
  - intro: doc tracks src/lib.rs (as-built)
  - Safety requirement scoped: message path safe; attach is
    the one unsafe entry
  - Memory layout: atomic Header fields, why-all-atomic,
    magic-last/Acquire-first handshake, CACHE_LINE dual role
    (layout ABI vs false-sharing pad, aarch64 caveat)
  - Index scheme: full check documented as >= M (fail toward
    Full on peer corruption)
  - API (was API sketch): real init/attach/split signatures,
    u64 sizing rationale, why attach is unsafe, geometry
    snapshot guarantee, per-call type assert + typed-endpoint
    follow-on
  - Validation: Miri (non-threaded) per commit, wrap test,
    loom as follow-on
  - Open questions -> Resolved questions: trust model (no UB
    from a hostile peer), split once-guard direction (CAS
    claims word)
- notes/todo.md: Todo 1 endpoint claims word, 2 typed
  endpoints; new Ideas section (wakeup, loom, polish, packed
  slots); mark -5 (current)
- Cargo.toml: bump to 0.3.0-5
- notes/chores/chores-01.md: backfill Commits: with [[6]]
  (the -4 SHA)
```

Title is 45 chars, matches the ladder line. I'll flip `-5` to `(done)` before pushing. OK to commit and push?

1	# Notes
2	
3	This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
4	Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.
5	
6	By default there are chores/chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
7	about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.
8	The chores-NN files are numbered in sequence; as a chores file grows
9	large the next one is opened ([chores/chores-01.md](chores/chores-01.md)
10	is the active one in this template), and earlier ones are closed and
11	kept for history.
12	
13	
14	Examples chore file:
15	```
16	# Chores-01.md
17	 
18	General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
19	more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
20	how and why of a particular chore.
21	
22	## Create a binary that lists jj info 
23	
24	This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
25	and using `jj-lib`
26	```
27	
28	## Workflow and conventions
29	
30	Bot-facing workflow and conventions live in
31	[`../AGENTS.md`](../AGENTS.md):
32	
33	- [Notes file conventions](../AGENTS.md#notes-file-conventions)
34	  — Todo format, Reference numbering, Notes references
35	  (`[[N]]` citation style), Markdown anchor links, Retiring
36	  Done entries.
37	- [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions) —
38	  section headers / Done entries exact-title rule, content
39	  rules, `Commits:` line format.
40	- [Code Conventions](../AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc
41	  comments, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
42	  stuck detection.
43	
44	Per-cycle workflow lives in
45	[`cycle-protocol.md`](cycle-protocol.md):
46	
47	- [Cycles](cycle-protocol.md#cycles) — three-phase shape
48	  (Preparation → Work → Close-out), `X.Y.Z-N` numbering,
49	  sub-cycles.
50	- [Per-commit flow](cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
51	  cargo cycle (`fmt` / `clippy` / `test` / `install`),
52	  work + commit description review gates.
53	- [Commit description](cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
54	  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix; body shape
55	  per app vs `.claude` repo.
56	- [Pushing](cycle-protocol.md#pushing) — push policy,
57	  close-out shape, `.claude` cadence.
58	
59	This repo's versioning lives in
60	[`versioning.md`](versioning.md) — generic and shared
61	verbatim; the single source of truth that AGENTS.md and
62	cycle-protocol.md refer to abstractly:
63	
64	- [Terms](versioning.md#terms) — version / version-of-record /
65	  versioning.
66	- [Recording the version-of-record](versioning.md#recording-the-version-of-record)
67	  — manifest, notation, reporter, and cadence, by medium.
68	- [Step numbering](versioning.md#step-numbering) — the
69	  `X.Y.Z-N` scheme, nesting, optional Preparation.
70

# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores/chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.
The chores-NN files are numbered in sequence; as a chores file grows
large the next one is opened ([chores/chores-01.md](chores/chores-01.md)
is the active one in this template), and earlier ones are closed and
kept for history.


Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow and conventions live in
[`../AGENTS.md`](../AGENTS.md):

- [Notes file conventions](../AGENTS.md#notes-file-conventions)
  — Todo format, Reference numbering, Notes references
  (`[[N]]` citation style), Markdown anchor links, Retiring
  Done entries.
- [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions) —
  section headers / Done entries exact-title rule, content
  rules, `Commits:` line format.
- [Code Conventions](../AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc
  comments, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.

Per-cycle workflow lives in
[`cycle-protocol.md`](cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycles](cycle-protocol.md#cycles) — three-phase shape
  (Preparation → Work → Close-out), `X.Y.Z-N` numbering,
  sub-cycles.
- [Per-commit flow](cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  cargo cycle (`fmt` / `clippy` / `test` / `install`),
  work + commit description review gates.
- [Commit description](cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix; body shape
  per app vs `.claude` repo.
- [Pushing](cycle-protocol.md#pushing) — push policy,
  close-out shape, `.claude` cadence.

This repo's versioning lives in
[`versioning.md`](versioning.md) — generic and shared
verbatim; the single source of truth that AGENTS.md and
cycle-protocol.md refer to abstractly:

- [Terms](versioning.md#terms) — version / version-of-record /
  versioning.
- [Recording the version-of-record](versioning.md#recording-the-version-of-record)
  — manifest, notation, reporter, and cadence, by medium.
- [Step numbering](versioning.md#step-numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` scheme, nesting, optional Preparation.

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)

A completed dummy chore description.

## chore: dummy chore (TBD)

A dummy chore description.

## docs: versioning SSOT + generic conventions

Commits:

Adopt the converged generic convention set from fc, so the
template's `AGENTS.md`, `cycle-protocol.md`, and `versioning.md`
are byte-identical to fc's — future repos copy them verbatim and
edit nothing. The template gains the versioning SSOT and the
push-approval carve-out.

- New `notes/versioning.md`: the versioning single source of
  truth. Its `Recording the version-of-record` section states
  Manifest / Notation / Reporter / Cadence as
  conditionals-by-medium, so the file is fully generic — no
  per-project block.
- `cycle-protocol.md` gains the Commits-backfill machinery and
  the "explicit scoped delegation waives the gates" push-approval
  carve-out; `Numbering` defers to versioning.md.
- `AGENTS.md` absorbs fc's generic blocks (Committing-vs-pushing,
  one-command-per-invocation, Use-jj, Conventional-commit shape)
  and drops the `(<version>)` title suffix.

## docs: template cleanup

Commits: [[1]]

Strip the vc-template-x1 boilerplate so the repo reads as its own
project rather than a template.

- `README.md` loses the template/cloning/assumptions sections and
  gains a one-line project intro (zero-copy ring buffer
  experiment 1).
- `notes/todo.md` drops the dummy template entries and their
  references; the ring-buffer design task becomes Todo #1.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**docs: zero-copy ring buffer design**

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](ring-buffer-design.md).

Plan:
- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design (done)
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints (done)
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design (done)
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate (done)
- 0.3.0-4 fix: ring buffer soundness + attach handshake (done)
- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built (done)
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve/peek [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Blocking/wakeup story for reserve/peek (futex, eventfd);
  spin-only today.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.


# References

-zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
-buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.

-- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design (done)
-- 0.3.0-4 fix: ring buffer soundness + attach handshake (done)
-- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built (done)

input.new_string:
## docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

Commits: [[2]],[[3]],[[4]],[[5]],[[6]],[[7]]

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](../ring-buffer-design.md)
(kept in sync with `src/lib.rs`).

### As-built ladder

- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate
- 0.3.0-4 fix: ring buffer soundness + attach handshake
- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design (close-out)

### Outcome

- Landed the design doc plus the no_std SPSC crate: M×N
  cache-line slots, free-running AtomicU32 indices,
  reserve/commit + peek/release guards over zerocopy typed
  views; 6 tests including a threaded stress and a u32-wrap
  proof.
- A max-effort review between -3 and -4 found three real
  issues — a Miri-confirmed Stacked Borrows violation in
  init, a 32-bit region-size overflow reachable from safe
  code, and an init/attach publish race — all fixed in -4;
  Miri (non-threaded) joined the per-commit validation
  ladder.
- Follow-ons recorded: endpoint claims word and typed
  endpoints as Todo 1/2; wakeup story, loom, polish, and
  packed slots under Ideas.

# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores/chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.
The chores-NN files are numbered in sequence; as a chores file grows
large the next one is opened ([chores/chores-01.md](chores/chores-01.md)
is the active one in this template), and earlier ones are closed and
kept for history.


Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow and conventions live in
[`../AGENTS.md`](../AGENTS.md):

- [Notes file conventions](../AGENTS.md#notes-file-conventions)
  — Todo format, Reference numbering, Notes references
  (`[[N]]` citation style), Markdown anchor links, Retiring
  Done entries.
- [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions) —
  section headers / Done entries exact-title rule, content
  rules, `Commits:` line format.
- [Code Conventions](../AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc
  comments, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.

Per-cycle workflow lives in
[`cycle-protocol.md`](cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycles](cycle-protocol.md#cycles) — three-phase shape
  (Preparation → Work → Close-out), `X.Y.Z-N` numbering,
  sub-cycles.
- [Per-commit flow](cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  cargo cycle (`fmt` / `clippy` / `test` / `install`),
  work + commit description review gates.
- [Commit description](cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix; body shape
  per app vs `.claude` repo.
- [Pushing](cycle-protocol.md#pushing) — push policy,
  close-out shape, `.claude` cadence.

This repo's versioning lives in
[`versioning.md`](versioning.md) — generic and shared
verbatim; the single source of truth that AGENTS.md and
cycle-protocol.md refer to abstractly:

- [Terms](versioning.md#terms) — version / version-of-record /
  versioning.
- [Recording the version-of-record](versioning.md#recording-the-version-of-record)
  — manifest, notation, reporter, and cadence, by medium.
- [Step numbering](versioning.md#step-numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` scheme, nesting, optional Preparation.

1	# zc-ring-x1
2	
3	Zero copy ring buffer experiment 1
4	
5	This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
6	a bot to help develop a the code.
7	
8	The beginnings of a tool to help is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
9	
10	## Releasing
11	
12	**`cargo install` lockfile gotcha.** If you install your project's
13	binary with `cargo install --path .`, also pass `--locked`. By
14	default `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves
15	dependencies from scratch, so it can silently build the binary from
16	a different — possibly broken — dependency graph than `cargo build`
17	/ `cargo test` ran against.
18	
19	| command | reads `Cargo.lock`? | re-resolves? |
20	| --- | --- | --- |
21	| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
22	| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
23	| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
24	| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
25	
26	There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
27	for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
28	cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).
29	
30	The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
31	upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
32	pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
33	per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
34	[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
35	[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).
36	
37	## jj Tips for Git Users
38	
39	This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
40	alongside git. New to jj? See
41	[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
42	[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).
43	
44	Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
45	force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
46	live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).
47	
48	## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers
49	
50	Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
51	to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo via an
52	`ochid` (Other Change ID) trailer — the defining mechanism of the
53	dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
54	the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
55	see
56	[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).
57	
58	## Contributing
59	
60	Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
61	canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
62	and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):
63	
64	- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
65	  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
66	  Close-out).
67	- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
68	  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix and per-repo body
69	  shape.
70	- [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
71	  the cargo cycle plus the Work and Commit-Description review
72	  gates.
73	- [Code Conventions](AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
74	  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
75	  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.
76	
77	Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
78	near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
79	details in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific
80	formatting rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).
81	
82	## License
83	
84	Licensed under either of
85	
86	- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
87	- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)
88	
89	### Contribution
90	
91	Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
92	for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
93	be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

# zc-ring-x1

Zero copy ring buffer experiment 1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help develop a the code.

The beginnings of a tool to help is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)

## Releasing

**`cargo install` lockfile gotcha.** If you install your project's
binary with `cargo install --path .`, also pass `--locked`. By
default `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves
dependencies from scratch, so it can silently build the binary from
a different — possibly broken — dependency graph than `cargo build`
/ `cargo test` ran against.

| command | reads `Cargo.lock`? | re-resolves? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |

There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).

The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).

## jj Tips for Git Users

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
alongside git. New to jj? See
[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).

Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo via an
`ochid` (Other Change ID) trailer — the defining mechanism of the
dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
  Close-out).
- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix and per-repo body
  shape.
- [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  the cargo cycle plus the Work and Commit-Description review
  gates.
- [Code Conventions](AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific
formatting rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

## License

Licensed under either of

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

# zc-ring-x1

input.new_string:
# zc-ring-x1

Zero copy ring buffer experiment 1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help develop the code.

The beginnings of a tool to help is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)

## Overview

A `no_std` SPSC (single-producer / single-consumer) ring buffer
over a caller-provided memory region, moving typed messages with
zero copying at the boundary — the producer writes through a
`&mut T` directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads through
a `&T` in place. Typed views are validated casts via
[zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy); the same `#[repr(C)]`
layout works between threads and, via shared memory, between
processes. The full design — requirements, memory layout, index
scheme, API, validation — lives in
[notes/ring-buffer-design.md](notes/ring-buffer-design.md), kept
in sync with `src/lib.rs`.

- M slots × N bytes, both caller-chosen: M a power of two, N a
  cache-line multiple, every slot cache-line aligned.
- Free-running `AtomicU32` indices, masked only at slot access;
  no sacrificial slot; lock-free with Acquire/Release pairing.
- Guard API: `reserve::<T>()` → write → `commit()` on the
  producer side; `peek::<T>()` → read → `release()` on the
  consumer side; dropping a guard abandons cleanly.
- IPC-ready: one contiguous region, no internal pointers, an
  init/attach handshake (magic published last, Release), and an
  all-atomic header so a misbehaving peer can corrupt data but
  never cause UB.
- Validated by tests (including a threaded stress and a
  u32-index-wrap proof) and [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri)
  on the non-threaded suite.

```rust
use zc_ring_x1::Ring;
use zerocopy::{FromBytes, Immutable, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};

#[derive(FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout, Immutable)]
#[repr(C)]
struct Msg {
    seq: u64,
    val: u64,
}

// Cache-line-aligned region: 192 B header + 4 slots × 64 B.
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct Region([u8; 448]);
let mut region = Region([0; 448]);

let (mut producer, mut consumer) =
    Ring::init(&mut region.0, 64, 4).unwrap().split();

let mut slot = producer.reserve::<Msg>().unwrap();
slot.seq = 1;
slot.val = 42;
slot.commit(); // publish to the consumer

let msg = consumer.peek::<Msg>().unwrap();
assert_eq!(msg.val, 42);
msg.release(); // slot is free for reuse
```

Status: an experiment. SPSC only; attaching to an existing
shared-memory region is `unsafe` (see `Ring::attach`); planned
hardening and follow-ons are tracked in
[notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md).

  cache-line multiple, every slot cache-line aligned.
- Free-running `AtomicU32` indices, masked only at slot access;
  no sacrificial slot; lock-free with Acquire/Release pairing.
- Guard API: `reserve::<T>()` → write → `commit()` on the
  producer side; `peek::<T>()` → read → `release()` on the
  consumer side; dropping a guard abandons cleanly.
- IPC-ready: one contiguous region, no internal pointers, an
  init/attach handshake (magic published last, Release), and an
  all-atomic header so a misbehaving peer can corrupt data but
  never cause UB.
- Validated by tests (including a threaded stress and a
  u32-index-wrap proof) and [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri)
  on the non-threaded suite.

```rust
use zc_ring_x1::Ring;
use zerocopy::{FromBytes, Immutable, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};

#[derive(FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout, Immutable)]
#[repr(C)]
struct Msg {
    seq: u64,
    val: u64,
}

// Cache-line-aligned region: 192 B header + 4 slots × 64 B.
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct Region([u8; 448]);
let mut region = Region([0; 448]);

let (mut producer, mut consumer) =
    Ring::init(&mut region.0, 64, 4).unwrap().split();

let mut slot = producer.reserve::<Msg>().unwrap();
slot.seq = 1;
slot.val = 42;
slot.commit(); // publish to the consumer

let msg = consumer.peek::<Msg>().unwrap();
assert_eq!(msg.val, 42);
msg.release(); // slot is free for reuse
```

Status: an experiment. SPSC only; attaching to an existing
shared-memory region is `unsafe` (see `Ring::attach`); planned
hardening and follow-ons are tracked in
[notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md).

# zc-ring-x1

Zero copy ring buffer experiment 1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help develop a the code.

The beginnings of a tool to help is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)

## Releasing

**`cargo install` lockfile gotcha.** If you install your project's
binary with `cargo install --path .`, also pass `--locked`. By
default `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves
dependencies from scratch, so it can silently build the binary from
a different — possibly broken — dependency graph than `cargo build`
/ `cargo test` ran against.

| command | reads `Cargo.lock`? | re-resolves? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |

There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).

The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).

## jj Tips for Git Users

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
alongside git. New to jj? See
[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).

Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo via an
`ochid` (Other Change ID) trailer — the defining mechanism of the
dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
  Close-out).
- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix and per-repo body
  shape.
- [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  the cargo cycle plus the Work and Commit-Description review
  gates.
- [Code Conventions](AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific
formatting rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

## License

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

# zc-ring-x1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help develop the code.

The beginnings of a tool to help is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)

## Overview

A `no_std` SPSC (single-producer / single-consumer) ring buffer
over a caller-provided memory region, moving typed messages with
zero copying at the boundary — the producer writes through a
`&mut T` directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads through
a `&T` in place. Typed views are validated casts via
[zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy); the same `#[repr(C)]`
layout works between threads and, via shared memory, between
processes. The full design — requirements, memory layout, index
scheme, API, validation — lives in
[notes/ring-buffer-design.md](notes/ring-buffer-design.md), kept
in sync with `src/lib.rs`.

- M slots × N bytes, both caller-chosen: M a power of two, N a
  cache-line multiple, every slot cache-line aligned.
- Free-running `AtomicU32` indices, masked only at slot access;
  no sacrificial slot; lock-free with Acquire/Release pairing.
- Guard API: `reserve::<T>()` → write → `commit()` on the
  producer side; `peek::<T>()` → read → `release()` on the
  consumer side; dropping a guard abandons cleanly.
- IPC-ready: one contiguous region, no internal pointers, an
  init/attach handshake (magic published last, Release), and an
  all-atomic header so a misbehaving peer can corrupt data but
  never cause UB.
- Validated by tests (including a threaded stress and a
  u32-index-wrap proof) and [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri)
  on the non-threaded suite.

```rust

use zerocopy::{FromBytes, Immutable, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};

#[derive(FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout, Immutable)]
#[repr(C)]
struct Msg {
    seq: u64,
    val: u64,
}

// Cache-line-aligned region: 192 B header + 4 slots × 64 B.
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct Region([u8; 448]);
let mut region = Region([0; 448]);

let (mut producer, mut consumer) =
    Ring::init(&mut region.0, 64, 4).unwrap().split();

let mut slot = producer.reserve::<Msg>().unwrap();
slot.seq = 1;
slot.val = 42;
slot.commit(); // publish to the consumer

let msg = consumer.peek::<Msg>().unwrap();
assert_eq!(msg.val, 42);
msg.release(); // slot is free for reuse

- **`README.md`** — new `## Overview`: what the crate is (no_std SPSC, zerocopy typed views, thread + shared-memory IPC), feature bullets, a **runnable example** (verified — it's committed as `examples/readme.rs` so clippy keeps it honest against API drift), and a status paragraph linking the design doc and todo. Also fixed the "a the code" typo and the stale `(version)` suffix line in Contributing.
- **`notes/todo.md`** — In Progress replaced with `_No cycle currently in progress._`; Done gains `docs: zero-copy ring buffer design [[1]]` pointing at the chores section.
- **`notes/chores/chores-01.md`** — the cycle's problem statement moved under its section header, plus `### As-built ladder` (all seven commits) and `### Outcome` (what landed, the review→fix story, follow-ons recorded); `Commits:` backfilled with `[[7]]` (the `-5` SHA).
- **`notes/README.md`** — pointer to the design doc; fixed its stale `(version)` suffix line too.
- **`Cargo.toml`** — version-of-record → bare `0.3.0`.

On close-out shape: the cycle was published incrementally (Keep separate), so those commits are immutable on `main` — the close-out pushes as one more normal commit, no squash/merge decision remains.

Close out the 0.3.0 cycle: bookkeeping plus a README

  Commits: with [[7]] (the -5 SHA)

```

## Testing

  (committed as [examples/readme.rs](examples/readme.rs) so

dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
  Close-out).
- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits title rules (no version suffix,
  distinct per step) and per-repo body shape.
- [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  the cargo cycle plus the Work and Commit-Description review
  gates.
- [Code Conventions](AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific
formatting rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

content.text:
That's a **real second bug, and your full-suite run caught what my `--skip threaded` run structurally couldn't** — the violation only manifests when both threads race, which is exactly the test I was skipping. Good catch. Let me explain what Miri found, then fix it properly.

**The bug:** `Reserved` holds `msg: &'p mut T` — a live Rust reference into the slot. When you call `slot.commit()`, the guard is passed *by value*, and Stacked Borrows puts a **protector** on the reference fields of that argument for the whole duration of `commit`. Inside that protected window, `commit` does the Release store of `producer_idx` — at which point the consumer thread may immediately Acquire-observe it and read the slot. That read is a foreign access to memory covered by a strongly-protected `&mut` → UB. `Peeked`/`release` has the mirror-image problem (producer may *write* the slot while `release`'s protected `&T` is still live). It's not a Miri false positive — a protected `&mut` is `noalias` to LLVM, so this is genuinely miscompilable. My review point about "the MutexGuard pattern" was wrong in one detail: `MutexGuard` holds a raw pointer to the data, not a `&mut T` field — that's precisely the fix here.

**Fix:** guards store raw pointers (`*mut T` / `*const T`) plus `PhantomData` for the borrow; `Deref`/`DerefMut` mint short-lived references on access (dead by the time `commit`/`release` runs, enforced by borrowck since `commit(self)` moves the guard). This also lets `reserve`/`peek` derive the pointer raw-to-raw from the slot base — the intermediate `&mut [u8]` slice and the zerocopy prefix-cast unwraps disappear; the zerocopy trait bounds stay as the validity proof. And I'll make the threaded test Miri-runnable (`COUNT = 200` under `cfg(miri)`) so the *full* suite runs under Miri from now on — no `--skip`.

This is a code fix, so it shouldn't ride the close-out commit (bookkeeping only). I'm inserting **`-6 fix: ring buffer guard aliasing under Miri`** before close-out: I'll temporarily restore the In Progress block, land `-6`, then redo the close-out edits. Implementing:

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve/peek [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Blocking/wakeup story for reserve/peek (futex, eventfd);
  spin-only today.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

## docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

Commits: [[2]],[[3]],[[4]],[[5]],[[6]],[[7]]

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](../ring-buffer-design.md)
(kept in sync with `src/lib.rs`).

### As-built ladder

- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate
- 0.3.0-4 fix: ring buffer soundness + attach handshake
- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design (close-out)

### Outcome

- Landed the design doc plus the no_std SPSC crate: M×N
  cache-line slots, free-running AtomicU32 indices,
  reserve/commit + peek/release guards over zerocopy typed
  views; 6 tests including a threaded stress and a u32-wrap
  proof.
- A max-effort review between -3 and -4 found three real
  issues — a Miri-confirmed Stacked Borrows violation in
  init, a 32-bit region-size overflow reachable from safe
  code, and an init/attach publish race — all fixed in -4;
  Miri (non-threaded) joined the per-commit validation
  ladder.
- Follow-ons recorded: endpoint claims word and typed
  endpoints as Todo 1/2; wakeup story, loom, polish, and
  packed slots under Ideas.

## docs: template cleanup

## docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

Commits: [[2]],[[3]],[[4]],[[5]],[[6]],[[7]]

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](../ring-buffer-design.md)
(kept in sync with `src/lib.rs`).

### As-built ladder

- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate
- 0.3.0-4 fix: ring buffer soundness + attach handshake
- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design (close-out)

### Outcome

- Landed the design doc plus the no_std SPSC crate: M×N
  cache-line slots, free-running AtomicU32 indices,
  reserve/commit + peek/release guards over zerocopy typed
  views; 6 tests including a threaded stress and a u32-wrap
  proof.
- A max-effort review between -3 and -4 found three real
  issues — a Miri-confirmed Stacked Borrows violation in
  init, a 32-bit region-size overflow reachable from safe
  code, and an init/attach publish race — all fixed in -4;
  Miri (non-threaded) joined the per-commit validation
  ladder.
- Follow-ons recorded: endpoint claims word and typed
  endpoints as Todo 1/2; wakeup story, loom, polish, and
  packed slots under Ideas.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# zc-ring-x1

Zero copy ring buffer experiment 1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help develop the code.

The beginnings of a tool to help is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)

## Overview

A `no_std` SPSC (single-producer / single-consumer) ring buffer
over a caller-provided memory region, moving typed messages with
zero copying at the boundary — the producer writes through a
`&mut T` directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads through
a `&T` in place. Typed views are validated casts via
[zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy); the same `#[repr(C)]`
layout works between threads and, via shared memory, between
processes. The full design — requirements, memory layout, index
scheme, API, validation — lives in
[notes/ring-buffer-design.md](notes/ring-buffer-design.md), kept
in sync with `src/lib.rs`.

- M slots × N bytes, both caller-chosen: M a power of two, N a
  cache-line multiple, every slot cache-line aligned.
- Free-running `AtomicU32` indices, masked only at slot access;
  no sacrificial slot; lock-free with Acquire/Release pairing.
- Guard API: `reserve::<T>()` → write → `commit()` on the
  producer side; `peek::<T>()` → read → `release()` on the
  consumer side; dropping a guard abandons cleanly.
- IPC-ready: one contiguous region, no internal pointers, an
  init/attach handshake (magic published last, Release), and an
  all-atomic header so a misbehaving peer can corrupt data but
  never cause UB.
- Validated by tests (including a threaded stress and a
  u32-index-wrap proof) and [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri)
  on the non-threaded suite.

```rust
use zc_ring_x1::Ring;
use zerocopy::{FromBytes, Immutable, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};

#[derive(FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout, Immutable)]
#[repr(C)]
struct Msg {
    seq: u64,
    val: u64,
}

// Cache-line-aligned region: 192 B header + 4 slots × 64 B.
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct Region([u8; 448]);
let mut region = Region([0; 448]);

let (mut producer, mut consumer) =
    Ring::init(&mut region.0, 64, 4).unwrap().split();

let mut slot = producer.reserve::<Msg>().unwrap();
slot.seq = 1;
slot.val = 42;
slot.commit(); // publish to the consumer

let msg = consumer.peek::<Msg>().unwrap();
assert_eq!(msg.val, 42);
msg.release(); // slot is free for reuse
```

Status: an experiment. SPSC only; attaching to an existing
shared-memory region is `unsafe` (see `Ring::attach`); planned
hardening and follow-ons are tracked in
[notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md).

## Testing

- `cargo test` — the full suite, including a threaded stress
  test and a u32-index-wrap test.
- `cargo run --example readme` — the Overview example above
  (committed as [examples/readme.rs](examples/readme.rs) so
  `cargo clippy --all-targets` keeps it compiling against the
  real API).
- `cargo +nightly miri test -- --skip threaded` — the
  non-threaded suite under
  [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri), which checks the
  `unsafe` code against the memory model (it caught a real
  Stacked Borrows bug in 0.3.0-4). One-time setup:
  `rustup component add --toolchain nightly miri`. The
  threaded stress test is skipped — 100k spin-loop iterations
  crawl under an interpreter.

## Releasing

**`cargo install` lockfile gotcha.** If you install your project's
binary with `cargo install --path .`, also pass `--locked`. By
default `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves
dependencies from scratch, so it can silently build the binary from
a different — possibly broken — dependency graph than `cargo build`
/ `cargo test` ran against.

| command | reads `Cargo.lock`? | re-resolves? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |

There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).

The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).

## jj Tips for Git Users

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
alongside git. New to jj? See
[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).

Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo via an
`ochid` (Other Change ID) trailer — the defining mechanism of the
dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
  Close-out).
- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits title rules (no version suffix,
  distinct per step) and per-repo body shape.
- [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  the cargo cycle plus the Work and Commit-Description review
  gates.
- [Code Conventions](AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific
formatting rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

## License

Licensed under either of

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

- `Peeked::release(self)` — frees the slot

Guard internals: both guards hold **raw slot pointers** and
mint `&T` / `&mut T` per access via Deref. A reference *field*
would be argument-protected (noalias) for the entire
`commit`/`release` call, but the store inside that call hands
the slot to the other side, which may access it while the
protector is still live — an aliasing race Miri flags
(found in 0.3.0-6 by the threaded test).

content.text:
Work for `0.3.0-6` is complete. Both validations green: cargo cycle 6/6, and the **full** Miri suite 6/6 — including `threaded_spsc`, which is the test that reproduced your failure. Please review. What changed:

**The fix (`src/lib.rs`):**
- `Reserved` and `Peeked` now hold **raw slot pointers** (`*mut T` / `*const T`) plus a `PhantomData` borrow marker; `Deref`/`DerefMut` mint short-lived references per access, with the SAFETY argument (check_type size/align, `FromBytes` validity, index-protocol exclusivity). By the time `commit(self)`/`release(self)` runs, no reference into the slot exists to be argument-protected — the race Miri flagged is structurally gone.
- `reserve`/`peek` derive the pointer raw-to-raw from the slot base; the intermediate slice, the zerocopy prefix-cast calls, and their two `unwrap`s are deleted (the zerocopy trait bounds remain as the message contract).
- `threaded_spsc` runs `COUNT = 200` under `cfg(miri)` (100k natively), so the full suite is Miri-runnable — about 1 second.

**Docs (riding this commit):** the README `## Overview`/`## Testing` you asked for (Testing now documents plain `cargo +nightly miri test`, the two bugs Miri caught, and the reduced-count note), the design doc's Validation section updated to "full suite, no skip" plus a new *Guard internals* paragraph explaining why the guards are raw-pointer-based, and the notes/README pointers.

**Choreography:** the close-out-only edits are reverted for now — In Progress is back with `-6` marked `(current)`, the chores section is bare again, Cargo.toml is `0.3.0-6`. Close-out gets redone cleanly after `-6` lands, and ends up purely bookkeeping.

One process note worth recording at close-out: `--skip threaded` was a coverage hole — the only concurrency-exercising test was excluded from the only tool that checks aliasing rules. The Outcome section will say so.

Want the commit description?

content.text:
**Title:** `fix: ring buffer guard aliasing under Miri`

**Body:**
```
Fix UB found by the user running the full Miri suite
(0.3.0-6): Reserved/Peeked held &mut T / &T fields, which are
argument-protected (noalias) for the whole commit()/release()
call — but the store inside that call hands the slot to the
other side, which can access it while the protector is live.
Guards now hold raw slot pointers and mint references per
access, so no slot reference exists when the handoff store
runs. Full Miri suite (threaded included) now passes; the
prior --skip threaded run was a coverage hole — the only
concurrency test was excluded from the only aliasing checker.

- src/lib.rs
  - Reserved/Peeked: msg is *mut T / *const T + PhantomData
    borrow marker; Deref/DerefMut mint short-lived refs with
    per-site SAFETY comments
  - reserve/peek: derive the slot pointer raw-to-raw; drop
    the intermediate slice, zerocopy prefix casts, and their
    unwraps (trait bounds stay as the message contract)
  - threaded_spsc: COUNT = 200 under cfg(miri) so the full
    suite is Miri-runnable (~1s)
- README.md: new Overview (features, runnable example,
  status links) and Testing (cargo test, example, full-Miri
  invocation + setup, two bugs caught); fix intro typo and
  stale (version)-suffix line
- examples/readme.rs: new — the README example as a compiled
  target so clippy pins it to the API
- notes/ring-buffer-design.md: Validation runs the full Miri
  suite (no skip); new Guard internals paragraph (why raw
  pointers)
- notes/README.md: design-doc pointer; fix stale
  (version)-suffix line
- notes/todo.md: add -6 ladder step (current)
- Cargo.toml: bump to 0.3.0-6
```

Title is 42 chars, matches the ladder line. I'll flip `-6` to `(done)` before pushing. OK to commit and push?

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**docs: zero-copy ring buffer design**

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](ring-buffer-design.md).

Plan:
- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design (done)
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints (done)
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design (done)
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate (done)
- 0.3.0-4 fix: ring buffer soundness + attach handshake (done)
- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built (done)
- 0.3.0-6 fix: ring buffer guard aliasing under Miri (current)
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve/peek [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Blocking/wakeup story for reserve/peek (futex, eventfd);
  spin-only today.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

input.new_string:
## docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

Commits: [[2]],[[3]],[[4]],[[5]],[[6]],[[7]],[[8]]

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](../ring-buffer-design.md)
(kept in sync with `src/lib.rs`).

### As-built ladder

- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate
- 0.3.0-4 fix: ring buffer soundness + attach handshake
- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built
- 0.3.0-6 fix: ring buffer guard aliasing under Miri
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design (close-out)

### Outcome

- Landed the design doc plus the no_std SPSC crate: M×N
  cache-line slots, free-running AtomicU32 indices,
  reserve/commit + peek/release guards over zerocopy-bounded
  typed views; 6 tests including a threaded stress and a
  u32-wrap proof; README overview with a compiled example.
- A max-effort review between -3 and -4 found three real
  issues — a Miri-confirmed Stacked Borrows violation in
  init, a 32-bit region-size overflow reachable from safe
  code, and an init/attach publish race — fixed in -4.
- The user running the *full* Miri suite found a fourth: the
  guards' reference fields are argument-protected during
  commit/release while the handoff store races them — fixed
  in -6 (guards hold raw pointers, references minted per
  access).
- Lesson recorded: -4's `--skip threaded` Miri run was a
  coverage hole — the only concurrency test was excluded from
  the only aliasing checker. The ladder now runs the full
  Miri suite (threaded count reduced under `cfg(miri)`).
- Follow-ons recorded: endpoint claims word and typed
  endpoints as Todo 1/2; wakeup story, loom, polish, and
  packed slots under Ideas.

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task

contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## docs: versioning SSOT + generic conventions

Commits:

Adopt the converged generic convention set from fc, so the
template's `AGENTS.md`, `cycle-protocol.md`, and `versioning.md`
are byte-identical to fc's — future repos copy them verbatim and
edit nothing. The template gains the versioning SSOT and the
push-approval carve-out.

- New `notes/versioning.md`: the versioning single source of
  truth. Its `Recording the version-of-record` section states
  Manifest / Notation / Reporter / Cadence as
  conditionals-by-medium, so the file is fully generic — no
  per-project block.
- `cycle-protocol.md` gains the Commits-backfill machinery and
  the "explicit scoped delegation waives the gates" push-approval
  carve-out; `Numbering` defers to versioning.md.
- `AGENTS.md` absorbs fc's generic blocks (Committing-vs-pushing,
  one-command-per-invocation, Use-jj, Conventional-commit shape)

## docs: template cleanup

Commits: [[1]]

Strip the vc-template-x1 boilerplate so the repo reads as its own
project rather than a template.

- `README.md` loses the template/cloning/assumptions sections and
  gains a one-line project intro (zero-copy ring buffer
  experiment 1).
- `notes/todo.md` drops the dummy template entries and their
  references; the ring-buffer design task becomes Todo #1.

## docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

Commits: [[2]],[[3]],[[4]],[[5]],[[6]],[[7]],[[8]]

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](../ring-buffer-design.md)
(kept in sync with `src/lib.rs`).

### As-built ladder

- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate
- 0.3.0-4 fix: ring buffer soundness + attach handshake
- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built
- 0.3.0-6 fix: ring buffer guard aliasing under Miri
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design (close-out)

### Outcome

- Landed the design doc plus the no_std SPSC crate: M×N
  cache-line slots, free-running AtomicU32 indices,
  reserve/commit + peek/release guards over zerocopy-bounded
  typed views; 6 tests including a threaded stress and a
  u32-wrap proof; README overview with a compiled example.
- A max-effort review between -3 and -4 found three real
  issues — a Miri-confirmed Stacked Borrows violation in
  init, a 32-bit region-size overflow reachable from safe
  code, and an init/attach publish race — fixed in -4.
- The user running the *full* Miri suite found a fourth: the
  guards' reference fields are argument-protected during
  commit/release while the handoff store races them — fixed
  in -6 (guards hold raw pointers, references minted per
  access).
- Lesson recorded: -4's `--skip threaded` Miri run was a
  coverage hole — the only concurrency test was excluded from
  the only aliasing checker. The ladder now runs the full
  Miri suite (threaded count reduced under `cfg(miri)`).
- Follow-ons recorded: endpoint claims word and typed
  endpoints as Todo 1/2; wakeup story, loom, polish, and
  packed slots under Ideas.

Outcome now records the fourth bug (your full-Miri run), the fix, and the lesson: `--skip threaded` excluded the only concurrency test from the only aliasing checker — the ladder now runs the full suite. `Commits:` backfilled with `[[8]]` (the `-6` SHA).
